title: When HR Is Not Your Friend
dek: Human Resources exists to protect the company, not your nervous system. When you realize the institution cannot hold your humanity, your body will tell you first. Here is how to listen to the fury in your fascia and reclaim your own protection.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Cold Room and the Hot Blood

You are sitting in a room with no windows, or perhaps it is a Zoom call where the background is blurred, but the feeling is the same. The air is sterile. The person across from you is nodding, using words like 'alignment' and 'culture fit' and 'moving forward.' But your body knows before your brain does. Your body always knows. The heat starts in your belly, a slow, dark simmer that rises up through your diaphragm. Your psoas muscle, the deep core of your physical being, tightens. It is preparing you to run, or to fight, but you are sitting perfectly still in an ergonomic chair. This is the moment you realize that Human Resources is not your friend. They are the immune system of the corporation, and right now, you are being treated as the pathogen.

Your jaw remembers this feeling. The masseter muscle, the strongest muscle in your body by weight, clamps down. You are biting back the truth because the truth is not welcome here. You have brought a grievance, a wound, a boundary violation, and it is being fed into a machine that only knows how to protect the bottom line. The fury you feel is not a moral failure. It is not a sign that you are unprofessional. It is biology. It is your nervous system screaming that you are in danger, that the social contract has been broken, that the people who claim to care for your well-being are actively managing your exit or your silence.

We are taught to suppress this. We are taught that anger in the workplace is ugly, especially if you are a woman, especially if you are marginalized. But the anger is a messenger. It is a brilliant, blazing messenger. When you push it down, it does not disappear. It settles into your levator scapulae, the muscles that hike your shoulders up to your ears. It settles into your gut, disrupting your digestion. It becomes a chronic ache, a tension headache, a sudden inability to sleep at three in the morning. The body keeps the score, as Bessel van der Kolk has taught us so profoundly. The trauma of institutional betrayal is not just in your mind; it is lodged in your tissues, waiting to be acknowledged.

## The Illusion of Safety

There is a specific smell to institutional betrayal. It smells like stale coffee and whiteboard markers and the faint, metallic tang of adrenaline in your own sweat. You thought you were safe. You read the employee handbook, you attended the wellness seminars, you believed the rhetoric about 'bringing your whole self to work.' But when you actually brought your whole self—when you brought your boundaries, your need for respect, your refusal to be diminished—the system recoiled. 

Gabor Maté speaks eloquently about the tension between attachment and authenticity. In the workplace, we are often forced to choose attachment—keeping the job, keeping the peace, staying in the good graces of authority—over our own authenticity. We swallow our truth to survive. But the cost of this suppression is immense. When we chronically suppress our anger, when we deny our own reality to appease a system that does not love us, we turn that aggressive energy inward. It becomes depression, anxiety, autoimmune flare-ups. Your body is attacking itself because it is not allowed to attack the true source of the threat.

You must understand this: HR is not a therapeutic space. It is a risk management department. When you sit in that chair and feel the cold sweat on your palms, your body is correctly assessing the environment. You are not crazy. You are not overreacting. Your nervous system is functioning perfectly. It is telling you that the smiling person taking notes is not your advocate. They are gathering data. And your fury, that hot, bright energy surging through your veins, is your advocate. It is the only thing in the room that is entirely on your side.

## The Somatic Reality of Betrayal

Let us talk about the fascia. The fascia is the connective tissue that wraps around every muscle, every organ, every nerve in your body. It is a continuous web of communication. When you experience a shock—like the shock of being gaslit by a manager, or the shock of HR telling you that your harassment complaint is actually a 'communication issue'—your fascia contracts. It hardens. It creates a physical armor to protect you from the blow.

This is why, after a meeting with HR, you might feel physically bruised. You might feel like you have been in a car accident. Your trapezius muscles are screaming. Your breath is shallow, trapped in the upper chest, unable to reach the deep, grounding space of your belly. You are in a state of sympathetic nervous system arousal—fight or flight—but you are trapped in a professional environment where neither fighting nor fleeing is permitted. So you freeze. You nod. You say 'thank you for your time.' And you carry that frozen terror home with you.

Resmaa Menakem, in his vital work on somatic abolitionism, reminds us that trauma is not just an event; it is a retention of energy in the body. The energy of your unexpressed anger, your unexpressed self-defense, gets stuck. It needs to move. If it does not move, it becomes a haunting. It becomes the reason you snap at your partner, the reason you cannot digest your food, the reason you wake up at 4:00 AM with your heart pounding out of your chest. The institution has invaded your body, and you must evict it.

## Herbal Allies for the Eviction

When the institution has invaded your nervous system, you need allies. You need medicine that is older and wiser than corporate policy. You need the earth. I am not talking about a polite cup of tea to make you docile. I am talking about herbs as fierce protectors, as boundary-makers, as deep nourishment for a depleted system.

Consider [Herb Pharm Stress Manager (1 oz)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005JW1F1C?tag=spankyspinola-20). Motherwort, *Leonurus cardiaca*, is the lion-hearted mother. It is for when your heart is racing with anxiety and rage, when you feel like you are going to jump out of your skin. It does not suppress the anger; it holds it. It gives your heart the structural support to process the fury without breaking. It is a bitter herb, and bitterness is what we need when we have been fed too many sweet lies. Bitterness stimulates the liver, the organ that Traditional Chinese Medicine associates with anger and the smooth flow of Qi. When your liver is stagnant with unexpressed rage, Motherwort helps to move it.

Then there is [FES Yarrow Environmental Solution Spray (2-pack)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00F3SKA7M?tag=spankyspinola-20). *Scutellaria lateriflora*. This is for the circular thinking, the endless looping of the conversation in your head. *What if I had said this? Why didn't I say that? How could they do this?* Skullcap is for the nervous system that is frayed at the edges, the nerves that feel like exposed wires. It brings a profound, grounding quiet to the mind, allowing the body to finally drop out of hyper-vigilance and into rest. It is not a sedative that knocks you out; it is a restorative that rebuilds the myelin sheath around your nerves.

And we must not forget [Herb Pharm Mind Soother (formerly Anxiety Soother)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B008CPAKO6?tag=spankyspinola-20). *Urtica dioica*. Nettle is a warrior plant. It stings. It has boundaries. When you have been depleted by a toxic work environment, when your adrenal glands are exhausted from pumping out cortisol day after day, Nettle rebuilds your blood. It is rich in iron, magnesium, and deep green vitality. Drinking a strong infusion of Nettle is like putting armor on from the inside out. It reminds your body of its own strength, its own resilience, its own right to take up space.

## The Practice of Reclaiming Your Body

You cannot think your way out of a somatic trauma. You cannot logic your way out of the betrayal of HR. You must move it through the tissue. Here is a practice for when you come home from that meeting, when the anger is vibrating in your jaw and your chest.

**The Jaw Release and the Primal Sound**

Find a space where you will not be interrupted. Stand with your feet hip-width apart, feeling the solid ground beneath you. The earth does not care about your job title. The earth holds you regardless. 

Bring your hands to your face. Find the masseter muscle—the thick muscle at the back of your jaw, just below your cheekbones. Press your fingers into it. It will likely be exquisitely tender. Do not shy away from the tenderness. Press in, and slowly open your mouth as wide as you can. Feel the resistance. Feel the holding. 

Now, take a deep breath into your belly. Not your chest—your belly. Let it expand like a balloon. As you exhale, let out a sound. Not a polite sigh. A groan. A growl. A sigh of profound disgust. Let the sound vibrate through your jaw, down your throat, and into your chest. Do this five times. With each exhale, imagine the sterile air of that HR office leaving your lungs. Imagine the corporate jargon being expelled from your cells. 

Shake your hands. Shake your arms. Shake your legs. Let your body tremble. Trembling is the nervous system's natural way of discharging excess survival energy. Animals do it in the wild after escaping a predator. You have just escaped a predator. Let your body shake it off.

## The Wisdom of the Boundary

When you realize that HR is not your friend, you are initiated into a deeper level of adulthood. It is a painful initiation, but it is a necessary one. You stop looking to the institution to parent you. You stop expecting a corporation to have a soul. And in doing so, you reclaim your own soul.

Pema Chödrön teaches us about the wisdom of no escape. We want to escape the discomfort of the conflict. We want someone else to fix it. We want HR to be the benevolent authority that makes everything right. But when we stay with the raw, burning discomfort of the reality—that we are on our own, that the system is rigged—we discover an indestructible core of sanity within ourselves.

Your anger is the guardian of that sanity. It is the fire that burns away the illusion. When you stop trying to be a 'good employee' and start being a fiercely embodied human being, your entire posture changes. You stop shrinking. You stop apologizing for your existence. You do your work, you collect your paycheck, but you do not give them your spirit. You keep your spirit for yourself, for your loved ones, for the earth.

This is the ultimate boundary. The boundary is not just what you say; it is how you inhabit your flesh. It is the refusal to let their metrics define your worth. It is the refusal to let their gaslighting rewrite your reality. Your body knows the truth. Trust the tension in your gut. Trust the heat in your face. Trust the fury. It is the most honest thing you have.

## Moving Forward Without Forgetting

There is a pressure in our culture to 'move on,' to 'let go,' to 'forgive and forget.' This is often weaponized against those who have been harmed by institutions. HR will tell you to 'look forward' and 'focus on the positive.' This is a demand for spiritual bypass. It is a demand that you amputate a part of your experience to make them comfortable.

Do not amputate your experience. Thich Nhat Hanh, the great Zen master, did not teach us to ignore our anger. He taught us to hold our anger like a crying baby. To say, 'Hello, my anger, I know you are there. I will take care of you.' You take care of your anger by listening to it, by validating it, by allowing it to inform your choices. 

You do not have to forgive the institution. Institutions do not require forgiveness; they require accountability, which they rarely face. What you must do is metabolize the experience so it does not poison you. You metabolize it through movement, through herbs, through speaking the truth to people who actually deserve to hear it. You metabolize it by refusing to internalize their dysfunction.

When you walk back into that building, or log back onto that server, you do so with your eyes wide open. You are no longer a naive participant in their family dynamic. You are a sovereign being, anchored in your own fascia, breathing into your own belly, protected by your own fierce boundaries. You are untouchable.

## The Anatomy of a Resilient Nervous System

Resilience is a word that has been co-opted by corporate wellness programs. They use it to mean 'how much abuse can you take before your productivity drops.' But true somatic resilience is entirely different. True resilience is the capacity of your nervous system to move fluidly between states of activation and rest. It is the ability to mount a massive, fiery defense when your boundaries are crossed, and then to return to a state of deep, restorative calm when the threat has passed.

When HR betrays you, your nervous system gets stuck in activation. You are constantly scanning for the next threat. Every email notification sends a jolt of cortisol through your veins. This is where the real work begins. The work is not to stop being angry. The work is to complete the stress cycle.

This means prioritizing your physical recovery with the same intensity that you prioritize your work. It means taking hot baths with Epsom salts to draw the tension out of your muscles. It means drinking bitter teas to support your liver. It means lying on the floor with your legs up the wall to drain the stagnant fluid from your extremities and signal to your brain that you are safe.

It means recognizing that your body is a landscape, and right now, that landscape has been scorched. You must tend to it. You must water it. You must plant new seeds. The institution will not do this for you. They will only extract. You must become the fierce, uncompromising steward of your own biology.

## The Deep Tissue of Truth

We must return to the fascia, because the fascia never forgets. When you walk into the fluorescent-lit corridors of a corporate office, your body is constantly reading the environment. Neuroception, a term coined by Stephen Porges, describes how our nervous system evaluates risk without our conscious awareness. Your neuroception is picking up on the micro-expressions of your manager, the passive-aggressive tone of an email, the subtle ways that power is wielded and weaponized. 

When HR tells you that your experience is invalid, they are demanding that you override your own neuroception. They are asking you to disconnect from your own sensory apparatus. This is a form of somatic violence. It creates a split between what your body knows to be true and what your mind is being forced to accept. This split is the birthplace of chronic pain, of dissociation, of the deep, aching fatigue that no amount of sleep can cure.

To heal from this, you must practice radical somatic loyalty. You must decide that your body's perception of reality is the ultimate authority. When your stomach clenches in a meeting, you do not tell yourself to calm down. You say to your stomach, 'I feel you. I know we are not safe here. I will protect us.' This internal dialogue repairs the split. It brings you back into wholeness. It is the antidote to institutional gaslighting. You become your own fierce advocate, your own HR department, rooted in the undeniable truth of your own flesh and bone.

## The Final Severing

Eventually, you may leave the job. Or you may stay, but you will leave the illusion. Either way, there is a severing that must happen. A cutting of the energetic cord between your solar plexus and the corporation.

In Traditional Chinese Medicine, the lungs are associated with grief and the metal element. The metal element is about cutting away what is no longer necessary. It is the sword of discernment. When you finally accept that HR is not your friend, there is a grief to it. A grief for the safety you thought you had. A grief for the energy you wasted trying to make them understand.

Breathe into your lungs. Feel the expansion of your ribcage. With every exhale, imagine a sharp, silver sword cutting the invisible threads that tie your self-worth to their evaluation. Cut the thread of their approval. Cut the thread of their gaslighting. Cut the thread of their false promises.

You are free. You are angry, and you are grieving, and you are free. Your body belongs to you again. Your fury belongs to you. Your peace belongs to you. Guard it fiercely. Let the herbs support you. Let the earth hold you. Let your anger be the sacred fire that lights your way home.

## FAQ

**Q: What if I have to go to HR because of a legal or policy issue?**
**A:** Go, but go with your armor on. Document everything. Bring a witness if you can. Do not go seeking empathy, validation, or justice. Go to create a paper trail. Treat it as a tactical operation, not a therapeutic encounter. Breathe into your belly, keep your feet flat on the floor, and remember that they are protecting the company, not you.

**Q: I feel so exhausted after dealing with workplace conflict. Is this normal?**
**A:** It is entirely normal. It is biology. Your body has been pumping out survival hormones—adrenaline and cortisol—to help you navigate a threat. When the immediate threat passes, the hormone crash leaves you depleted. Your adrenal glands are exhausted. This is the time for deep rest, Nettle infusions, and refusing to push through the fatigue. Your exhaustion is a biological imperative. Listen to it.

**Q: How do I stop the angry thoughts from looping in my head at night?**
**A:** The looping thoughts are your brain trying to solve a somatic problem. The energy is stuck in your body, so your brain spins its wheels trying to discharge it. You must move the energy physically. Get out of bed. Shake your body. Do the jaw release practice. Drink a cup of Skullcap tea. Tell your body, 'We are safe right now in this room. We will fight the battle tomorrow.' You cannot think your way out of a somatic loop; you must feel your way out.

**Q: Is it okay to just quit if the environment is too toxic?**
**A:** Yes. Leaving a toxic environment is not a failure; it is a profound act of self-preservation. If your body is breaking down, if your digestion is ruined, if your sleep is shattered, your body is telling you that the cost of staying is too high. Trust your body. The institution will replace you in a week. You only have one nervous system. Protect it at all costs.
